topic: Slices - fill, fill_with, copy_from_slice, and copy_within

turns.content: I'm trying to initialize a Vec<u8> for an image buffer in my side project. I used .fill(0) but the docs say that sets all elements including the length field which isn't what I want, so did I make a mistake or is there a better way?

You are correct to be concerned. The .fill(value) method on slices actually writes that value into every byte of the slice including the length field at the end, which corrupts your vector's internal state. You should avoid using fill for Vec<u8> initialization and instead use .extend_from_slice or let the compiler initialize it to zero. If you need to set specific bytes later, you can use .copy_within or fill_with on a slice reference.

I didn't know that about length corruption, I thought fill only touched the data part since it's called on slices. But now I realize my buffer is broken because of this. Is there any way to safely use .fill if I really need to set all bytes including padding?

There is no safe way to use .fill directly on a Vec<u8> without breaking it, but you can work around this by first creating the vector with proper capacity and then using .copy_from_slice or manual iteration. If you need to fill every byte including padding for some reason, you should cast the slice away from the vector and apply .fill_with which calls your closure on each element individually rather than a simple value assignment.

That makes sense about closure vs simple assignment. I tried using fill_with but it seems to require an iterator over indices, not just the slice itself. Can't I just call .fill_with on my Vec<u8> directly without needing to extract a separate index range?

You cannot call fill_with directly on a Vec<u8> because it operates on slices and needs an iterator source. You must first create a slice using &mut self or &self, then pass that to fill_with along with your closure that takes indices and returns bytes. This lets you control which positions get set without corrupting the vector metadata.

Okay so I need to extract a slice first? But what if my buffer has trailing zeros from previous operations and I want to copy those over instead of re-filling with indices? Is there a method that handles overlapping regions like copy_within?

Yes, you can use .copy_within(src_range, dst) which is designed specifically for copying overlapping regions within the same slice without needing to extract or reallocate. For example if you want to move data from index 10 to index 5 in your buffer, you would call copy_within on a reference to that slice with ranges specifying source and destination indices.

topic: Slices - fill, fill_with, copy_from_slice, and copy_within

turns.content: Hey, I'm new to Rust and working on a big codebase. I've read about slices but I still don't feel confident using fill, fill_with, copy_from_slice, and copy_within. Can you help me get this straight?
Of course! Slices in Rust are really useful for working with arrays or vectors without copying data. Have you tried playing around with them yet? It’s great practice.
Yeah, I’ve seen slices used a bit, but when it comes to fill and friends, I get lost. Like, what's the difference between fill(value) and fill_with(f)?

Good question! `fill` is straightforward: it sets every element in the slice to a single value. So if you have a `[u8; 10]`, calling `.fill(42)` will make all elements `42`. It’s like saying, 'set everything here to this one thing.'
Ah, okay. And what about fill_with? I see it takes a closure now?

Exactly! `fill_with` lets you generate each element dynamically with a function. So if you want different values, you can do something like `.fill_with(|| rand::random::<u8>())` to fill the slice with random bytes. It’s more flexible when you need per-element logic.
Hmm, that makes sense. But wait—what about copy_from_slice? What do I have to watch out for?

Good point! `copy_from_slice` is great when you want to duplicate a slice into another one, but only if they’re the same length. If lengths differ, Rust panics (runtime error). So always check that both slices have the same size before calling it.
Right now I'm confused—what’s the difference between copy_from_slice and just copying a slice manually?

Good question! If you want to copy elements one by one, you’d need a loop or iterators. But `copy_from_slice` is optimized for bulk operations — it uses unsafe pointer tricks internally for speed. So if your slices are large and performance matters, prefer `copy_from_slice`. Otherwise, manual loops might be clearer.
Okay, so what about copy_within? When do I use that?

`copy_within` is perfect for moving parts of a slice around without creating copies or shifting elements. Imagine you have `[1, 2, 3, 4]`, and you want to move the first two elements after the last one: `slice.copy_within(0..2, 4)`. Result? `[1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2]`. It’s very handy for in-place rearrangements.
Oh, I see. So if I want to move elements from the beginning to the end of a slice without overwriting anything else, `copy_within` is ideal?
Exactly! It’s especially useful for circular or overlapping data transformations. Just be careful with ranges and destination indices — get it wrong, and you’ll overwrite parts by accident.
I think I’m starting to get it. So, if I want to fill a slice with all `0`s and then copy some elements from another vector without panics, what would be the safest way?

You’d first check that both slices are of equal length (or handle possible errors). Then you can use `.fill(0)` to initialize everything, and then `copy_from_slice` to duplicate data. This way, you avoid panics and make your code safer.
Great! Now I feel confident about fill, fill_with, copy_from_slice, and copy_within. Thanks for explaining it so clearly!
You're welcome! If you have any more questions as you dive deeper into Rust or anything else, just ask. Happy coding!
